    The Grill Execute Clear Loop Solution

    I want to add a lesson comments feature to my course platform. I'm not entirely sure of the scope, but I want to reach version 1 quickly and see something working.

    The minimum viable feature: students can comment on lessons, and instructors can also comment on those lessons.

    I start by running /grill-me with this prompt:

    I want to add a lesson comments feature. I'm not entirely sure of the scope, but I want to make sure we reach v1 pretty quickly, and so I want to see something working. The minimum is I definitely want students to be able to comment on lessons and have instructors also comment on those lessons.

    The agent reads my codebase and does a quick lay of the land.

    It has found:

    • A React Router course app with students, instructors, and admins
    • Per-lesson routes at courses.$slug.lessons.$lessonId.tsx
    • Lessons live under modules under courses
    • No existing comments-like table

    The agent now guides me through a series of design questions. Each question forces me to think deeply about a specific aspect of the feature before implementation begins.

    Who can comment?

    The agent recommends that only enrolled students and the course instructor can post and read comments. Comments are scoped to one lesson and only visible to people with access to that lesson.

    I accept this recommendation.

    Threading model

    Next, the agent asks about threading. Should I have:

    • Pure flat comments
    • One-level replies (replies to comments, but replies cannot be replied to)
    • Fully nested threads (like Twitter)

    The agent recommends one-level replies as a balanced approach for version 1. But I push back with a great question: what's reversible?

    I ask the agent which threading model would be hard to roll back from.

    The agent's answer is illuminating. It ranks the reversibility:

    OptionReversibility
    Pure flat → one-levelTrivial (add nullable parentId column later)
    One-level → fully nestedEasy (column exists, only UI changes)
    Fully nested → anything simplerHard (data meaning depends on depth)

    This flips the recommendation. Pure flat is the most reversible starting point. I accept this.

    Content format

    The agent recommends plain text with auto-linkified URLs. No rich text WYSIWYG for version 1. I agree.

    Editing and deleting

    The agent recommends:

    • Authors can delete their own comments
    • Instructors can delete any comments on their course's lessons
    • No editing in version 1
    • Use soft delete (a deletedAt column) so threads don't lose context
    • Deleted comments render as [deleted] placeholder

    I accept this recommendation.

    Where comments live

    The agent recommends a "Discussion" section appended below the lesson content and quiz. Same scroll, no tab, comments load with the lesson.

    I accept this.

    Sort order

    The agent recommends oldest-first (chronological). Discussions read like a Q&A thread, context flows top-to-bottom, instructor replies follow the question they answer.

    I accept this.

    Instructor badge

    The agent asks if instructor comments should have a visual distinction. It recommends an "Instructor" badge next to the instructor's name plus a subtle background tint.

    I accept this.

    Pagination

    The agent recommends no pagination for version 1, load all comments per lesson. Realistic v1 volume is small. If a lesson grows past ~50 comments later, I can add pagination then. It's fully reversible.

    I accept this.

    Notifications

    The agent recommends out of scope for v1. No notifications. Notifications mean either email infrastructure or a notifications table with in-app UI, both are multi-day add-ons.

    I accept this.

    Character limit

    The agent recommends 2000 characters, enforced server-side. Long enough for detailed questions or code-pasted answers; short enough to keep comments scannable.

    I accept this.

    Moderation

    The agent recommends deferring all moderation (reporting, rate limiting) for v1. But it suggests one small extra: allow admins to delete comments too. It's one extra OR clause, zero schema changes.

    I disagree with the base recommendation and accept this suggestion. Admin delete just makes sense.

    Benefits of focused questioning

    As I work through this process, I only have one question to deal with at a time. My focus shifts from question to question, resolving each one in line.

    If I had to plan all of this at once in a big document, I'd probably get lazy or deprioritize some decisions. But taking time to think through each decision is crucial. It ensures I get really good alignment in the output.

    I notice something else: I'm not just passively answering. I'm actively driving the conversation. I asked a follow-up question about reversibility. I pushed back on the moderation recommendation. This isn't a one-way interrogation, it's a dialogue.

    The v1 spec

    After resolving all the consequential branches, I have a clear v1 spec:

    Scope

    • Enrolled students + course instructor + admins can read and post on a lesson's comments
    • Flat list (no threading)
    • Plain text, 2000 character limit, server-enforced
    • Auto-linkify URLs at render time
    • Sort oldest-first
    • Instructor comments get an "Instructor" badge plus subtle tint
    • Author can delete own; instructor can delete any on their course; admin can delete any
    • Soft delete (deletedAt); deleted comments render as [deleted] placeholder
    • No editing, no notifications, no reporting, no rate limit, no pagination

    Schema

    • New comments table with id, lessonId, userId, content, createdAt, deletedAt

    Code surface

    • New table + Drizzle migration
    • commentService.ts with listForLesson, create, softDelete (plus tests)
    • Extend the existing action in courses.$slug.lessons.$lessonId.tsx with create-comment and delete-comment intents
    • Extend the loader to include comments and author info
    • Append a <Discussion> section to the lesson page below content and quiz

    I've never created a plan asset. I've never formally written this down. I just had a conversation, and now I have complete alignment on what I'm building.

    Implementation begins

    I tell the agent to implement. It starts by updating the database schema.

    The agent:

    1. Adds the lessonComments table to app/db/schema.ts
    2. Runs pnpm db:generate to generate types
    3. Runs pnpm db:migrate to apply the migration

    The migration hits an error (the table already exists from a previous attempt), but the agent cleans this up by dropping the old table and re-migrating.

    Next, the agent implements the comment service in app/services/commentService.ts with listCommentsForLesson, createComment, and softDeleteComment functions. All 10 tests pass.

    Now the agent wires up the lesson route in courses.$slug.lessons.$lessonId.tsx. It updates:

    1. The loader to fetch comments (if the user can access the discussion)
    2. The action to handle create-comment and delete-comment intents
    3. The component to render a <Discussion> section below the lesson content

    The Discussion component includes a textarea for posting, a character counter, delete buttons for permitted users, and an "Instructor" badge on instructor comments.

    The agent runs typecheck and the full test suite. All 288 tests pass, including 10 new ones for the comment service. Typecheck is clean.

    Testing the comments feature

    I start the dev server. I log in as Emma Wilson, a student, and navigate to a lesson.

    I see a "Discussion" section at the bottom of the lesson page. I post a comment: "hey, like this". The comment appears in the discussion list.

    Next, I log in as Marcus Johnson, the course instructor. I navigate to the same lesson.

    I can see Emma's comment. I can delete it (because I'm the instructor). I can post my own comment. When I post as the instructor, my comment appears with an "Instructor" badge and a tinted background.

    I test that:

    • I can delete my own comments
    • I can delete Emma's comment (because I'm the instructor)
    • Deleted comments show as [deleted]
    • The deletion is a soft delete (the comment still exists in the database)

    I switch to James Park, a student not enrolled in this course. He doesn't see the comments at all.

    I switch to Olivia, another enrolled student. She can see the comments and can post, but she can't delete Emma's or Marcus's comments.

    Everything works perfectly.

    Results and takeaways

    I have a perfectly aligned implementation that matches what I planned. I didn't have to do any steering during implementation. I didn't even create a plan asset or a formal specification.

    I just had a conversation with the agent, thought through the design decisions, and then produced a working feature.

    This is the /grill-me workflow:

    1. Start with a rough idea
    2. Answer focused design questions one at a time
    3. Ask follow-up questions to drive the conversation
    4. Achieve complete alignment on scope and design
    5. Implement with confidence

    If you want to take this further, you can redo this entire lesson with plan mode and see if you have a different experience. But the core workflow is incredibly powerful.

    Nice work, I'll see you in the next one.
